questions.prompt: In Glean's vibe-code agent builder, how do you start building an agent?
options: By filling out a multi-page configuration form
By describing the goal in plain language and reading the drafted plan
By writing the agent in Rego
By importing a YAML file
questions.explanation: The Fall '25 launch described a conversational builder: you state the goal, it drafts a plan, and you review and refine it.

questions.prompt: The vibe-code builder, 100+ native actions, scheduled triggers, looping, and versioning are best described as...
options: Long-standing GA features you can rely on today
Beta / announcement-sourced from Glean's Fall '25 launch — verify GA in your tenant first
Open-source tools maintained outside Glean
Deprecated features being removed
questions.explanation: These were announced at Glean's Fall '25 launch and are Beta / announcement-sourced. Confirm GA availability in your own tenant before relying on them.

questions.prompt: Before adding a native WRITE action (e.g., update a Salesforce case) to an agent step, what should you check?
options: Nothing — write actions are always safe
Whose identity it acts as and what it is allowed to change
Only whether the app's icon is shown
Whether the action is alphabetically first
questions.explanation: Reads are cheap to trust; writes carry real authority. Mapping each write action to its permissions is the MAP discipline behind choosing actions.

questions.prompt: An agent runs unattended on a daily schedule. Which guard most directly keeps a bad run from compounding?
options: Running it more often
An empty-run no-op plus a loud failure path that reports errors
Removing the schedule entirely
Letting failures stay silent so logs stay clean
questions.explanation: A schedule repeats a mistake on a timer. An empty-run no-op and a visible failure path are the MANAGE controls that stop an unattended cadence from compounding.

questions.prompt: Why does rolling an agent back to a known-good version count as a governance control?
options: It makes the agent run faster
It bounds blast radius — a bad change is recoverable by policy, not by luck
It hides the change history from auditors
It deletes the older versions automatically
questions.explanation: Versioning makes every edit named, comparable, and reversible. Rollback bounds the blast radius of a bad change, which is a GOVERN-level guarantee.
